title: Sāṃkhyakārikā [Sāṃkhyasaptati]
author: Īśvarakṛṣṇa
date_estimate: c. 350-450 CE (Gupta era); some scholars argue as early as c. 200 CE
summary: The earliest surviving systematic text of the Sāṃkhya school, consisting of roughly 72 verses in the ārya metre (hence the alternative title Sāṃkhyasaptati, 'the seventy [verses] of Sāṃkhya'). It expounds Sāṃkhya's dualist metaphysics: the distinction between unconscious primordial matter (prakṛti) and the plurality of conscious selves (puruṣa), the evolution of the twenty-five principles (tattvas), the three guṇas, the theory of causation (satkāryavāda), and liberation through discriminative knowledge.
history: Īśvarakṛṣṇa presents himself as an heir to a teaching lineage descending from the sage Kapila through Āsuri and Pañcaśikha. The work became the foundational text of classical Sāṃkhya and attracted numerous commentaries, including the Yuktidīpikā, Gauḍapāda's bhāṣya, Vācaspati Miśra's Sāṃkhyatattvakaumudī, and the Sāṃkhyavṛtti; a Chinese translation by Paramārtha (6th c.) helps fix its date.

title: Sāṃkhyakārikā [Sāṃkhyasaptati]
author: Īśvarakṛṣṇa
date_estimate: c. 350-450 CE (Gupta era); possibly as early as c. 200 CE
summary: A second e-text instance of the same root work, Īśvarakṛṣṇa's Sāṃkhyakārikā, the earliest extant systematic statement of classical Sāṃkhya philosophy in about 72 ārya verses. It sets out the dualism of prakṛti and puruṣa, the twenty-five tattvas, the three guṇas, satkāryavāda, and liberation by discrimination. This record likely corresponds to a particular commentarial recension (its work_id appears to reference the Jayamaṅgalā commentary).
history: The Sāṃkhyakārikā is the locus classicus of Sāṃkhya and survives with several early commentaries; the Jayamaṅgalā is one of the older glosses (sometimes attributed to a Śaṅkara/Śaṅkarārya, distinct from Ādi Śaṅkara). Paramārtha's 6th-century Chinese rendering of the kārikā and a commentary provides a key chronological anchor.

title: Brahmasūtra (Śaṅkarabhāṣya)
author: Śaṅkara (Ādi Śaṅkarācārya); root sūtras attributed to Bādarāyaṇa
date_estimate: Śaṅkara's commentary c. early 8th c. CE (traditionally c. 788-820 CE); root Brahmasūtra c. 2nd c. BCE - 2nd c. CE
summary: Śaṅkara's commentary (bhāṣya) on the Brahmasūtra (Vedāntasūtra) of Bādarāyaṇa, the foundational systematic treatise of Advaita Vedānta. Across four chapters it interprets the terse aphorisms to argue that Brahman is the sole non-dual reality, that the individual self (ātman) is identical with Brahman, that the empirical world is grounded in māyā/avidyā, and it refutes rival schools (Sāṃkhya, Nyāya, Buddhism, etc.).
history: The Brahmasūtrabhāṣya is regarded as Śaṅkara's most important authenticated work and the cornerstone of Advaita. It generated a vast subcommentarial literature, most notably Vācaspati Miśra's Bhāmatī and Padmapāda's Pañcapādikā, which gave rise to the two main Advaita exegetical schools (Bhāmatī and Vivaraṇa).
related_works: Bhāṣyas of Rāmānuja and Madhva on the same sūtras

title: Jīvanmuktiviveka
author: Vidyāraṇya (formerly Mādhava)
summary: A treatise on jīvanmukti, 'liberation while still living,' examining the nature, means, and conduct of one who has attained release before bodily death. Drawing on Vedānta together with yogic disciplines, it discusses the dissolution of mind (manonāśa), destruction of latent tendencies (vāsanākṣaya), and the lifestyle of the liberated sage.
history: Composed by Vidyāraṇya, the Advaita scholar and Jagadguru of Śṛṅgeri (originally known as Mādhava), after he became a sannyāsin. The work exemplifies a 'yogic Advaita' that supplements Śaṅkara's emphasis on knowledge with disciplines drawn from the Yogasūtra, Bhagavadgītā, Gauḍapāda's Kārikā, and the Laghu-Yogavāsiṣṭha.

title: Bhāmatī
author: Vācaspati Miśra
date_estimate: 9th-10th c. CE (c. 840 CE, or by an alternate calendar reckoning c. 976 CE)
summary: Vācaspati Miśra's sub-commentary (ṭīkā) on Śaṅkara's Brahmasūtrabhāṣya. It elucidates and defends Śaṅkara's Advaita reading of the Brahmasūtra and develops distinctive positions, notably locating avidyā (nescience) in the individual jīva rather than in Brahman.
history: The Bhāmatī is so influential that it gave its name to one of the two principal schools of post-Śaṅkara Advaita exegesis (the 'Bhāmatī school,' as opposed to the Vivaraṇa school). It itself attracted further sub-commentaries, including Amalānanda's Kalpataru and Appayya Dīkṣita's Parimala. Vācaspati, a polymath who wrote authoritative works across several darśanas, dated one work to 840 CE (Saṃvat reckoning), the basis for his chronology.

title: Sarva-darśana-saṃgraha
author: Mādhava (commonly identified with Vidyāraṇya); attribution discussed
tradition: Hindu (Advaita Vedānta standpoint, surveying all schools)
date_estimate: c. 1370-1386 CE (later 14th c.)
summary: An encyclopedic survey ('compendium of all philosophical systems') that expounds the major Indian darśanas one by one, beginning with the materialist Cārvāka and proceeding through Buddhist, Jaina, and various Hindu systems up to Advaita Vedānta. The schools are arranged in an ascending hierarchy, each presented in its own terms before being superseded, culminating in the author's own Advaita as the highest truth.
history: Traditionally ascribed to Mādhavācārya, generally identified with Vidyāraṇya of Śṛṅgeri, though the attribution and the exact relation of 'Mādhava' to Vidyāraṇya have been debated by scholars. The work is a major source for our knowledge of medieval Indian philosophy, preserving accounts of schools (and even now-lost works) from a learned 14th-century vantage point.

title: Atharvaveda-Saṃhitā
author: anonymous/traditional (Vedic seers; Śaunaka recension)
date_estimate: c. 1200-900 BCE (composite; later than the Ṛgveda)
summary: The fourth Veda, a collection of hymns, charms, spells, and incantations addressing everyday life: healing of diseases, protection from demons and enemies, love and marriage, prosperity, kingship, and expiation, alongside more speculative cosmological and philosophical hymns. This record corresponds to the standard Śaunaka recension (the vulgate of the Atharvaveda).
history: Distinct in character from the three older 'sacrificial' Vedas, the Atharvaveda was accepted only later into the orthodox canon. It survives chiefly in two recensions, Śaunaka and Paippalāda. Its magical and medical material is a key source for early Indian folk religion and medicine (the roots of Āyurveda).

title: Atharvaveda-Saṃhitā
author: anonymous/traditional (Vedic seers)
date_estimate: c. 1200-900 BCE (composite; later than the Ṛgveda)
summary: A second e-text instance of the Atharvaveda-Saṃhitā, the fourth Veda comprising hymns, charms, and incantations for healing, protection, prosperity, love, and statecraft, together with cosmological and speculative hymns. This duplicate likely represents a different digital edition or recension (Śaunaka or Paippalāda) of the same corpus.
history: The Atharvaveda was admitted into the canon later than the Ṛg-, Yajur-, and Sāmaveda and exists in the Śaunaka and Paippalāda recensions. It is the principal Vedic witness to early Indian magic, medicine, and domestic ritual.

title: Sāmaveda-Saṃhitā
author: anonymous/traditional (Vedic seers)
date_estimate: c. 1200-1000 BCE (composite)
summary: The 'Veda of melodies/chants,' the third Veda, consisting largely of verses (mostly drawn from the Ṛgveda) arranged and set to melodies (sāman) for liturgical singing by the udgātṛ priests during the soma sacrifice. It comprises an ārcika (verse collection) and gāna (song-books) that indicate how the verses are to be chanted, and is foundational to the Indian musical and chant tradition.
history: Because most of its text is borrowed from the Ṛgveda, the Sāmaveda's importance lies in its musical notation and performance practice rather than new content. It survives in several recensions (Kauthuma, Rāṇāyanīya, Jaiminīya) and is the source from which Indian classical music traces its origins.

title: Ṛgveda
author: anonymous/traditional (Vedic ṛṣis of various clans)
date_estimate: c. 1500-1200 BCE for the core (composite); the oldest Vedic text
summary: The oldest of the four Vedas and one of the oldest extant texts in any Indo-European language, comprising 1,028 hymns (sūktas) in ten books (maṇḍalas) addressed mainly to deities such as Indra, Agni, Soma, Varuṇa, and the Aśvins. Alongside ritual invocations it contains cosmogonic, philosophical, and gnomic hymns (e.g. the Puruṣasūkta and the Nāsadīya 'Creation Hymn'). This record ('rvh') corresponds to a metrically restored/Holland-Van Nooten-style edition.
history: Composed and transmitted orally with extraordinary fidelity over millennia, the Ṛgveda is the cultural and religious bedrock of the Indian tradition and a primary source for Indo-Aryan history and Proto-Indo-European studies. Its family books (II-VII) are the oldest stratum, with books I and X added later.

title: Gopathabrāhmaṇa
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed to Gopatha)
date_estimate: later Vedic period, c. 800-600 BCE; one of the latest Brāhmaṇas (some place it later still)
summary: The only Brāhmaṇa associated with the Atharvaveda, a prose text in two parts (pūrva- and uttara-bhāga) that expounds rituals, priestly duties, cosmology, the symbolism of the sacred syllable Oṃ, and the dignity of the Brahman priest and the Atharvaveda itself. It is linked to both the Śaunaka and Paippalāda schools.
history: Regarded as one of the latest Brāhmaṇa texts and partly drawing on Vedāṅga material, the Gopathabrāhmaṇa was composed in part to assert the Atharvaveda's parity with the three older Vedas. Its exact date and authorship are debated.

title: Śatapatha-Brāhmaṇa
author: anonymous/traditional (associated with Yājñavalkya in parts)
date_estimate: c. 800-600 BCE (later Vedic period)
summary: The 'Brāhmaṇa of a hundred paths,' the great prose commentary attached to the White (Śukla) Yajurveda, describing in detail the major Vedic sacrifices (Agnihotra, new- and full-moon rites, Agnicayana fire-altar construction, Rājasūya, Aśvamedha, etc.). Beyond ritual mechanics it gives myths, etymologies, cosmogony (including a version of the flood/Manu legend), and philosophical reflection, and its final book overlaps with the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad.
history: Transmitted in two recensions, Mādhyandina and Kāṇva, it is one of the most important and extensive Brāhmaṇas, central to the study of Vedic ritual and early Indian thought; portions are associated with the sage Yājñavalkya. The Agnicayana sections are notable for early geometry/astronomy.

title: 13 Upaniṣads
author: anonymous/traditional (various Vedic seers)
date_estimate: c. 800-200 BCE for the principal Upaniṣads (composite)
summary: A collection of the principal (mukhya) Upaniṣads, the philosophical capstones of the Vedas (Vedānta) concerned with Brahman, ātman, karma, rebirth, and liberation. The set of thirteen is the standard scholarly grouping of the oldest and most authoritative Upaniṣads (e.g. Īśa, Kena, Kaṭha, Praśna, Muṇḍaka, Māṇḍūkya, Taittirīya, Aitareya, Chāndogya, Bṛhadāraṇyaka, Śvetāśvatara, Kauṣītaki, Maitrī).
history: These are the Upaniṣads on which the classical Vedānta tradition (Śaṅkara, Rāmānuja, etc.) wrote commentaries and from which the Brahmasūtra draws; they form the bedrock of Indian idealist and non-dualist philosophy. R. E. Hume's well-known translation collected 'The Thirteen Principal Upanishads,' the likely referent here.

title: 112 Upaniṣads
author: anonymous/traditional (various)
tradition: Hindu (Vedānta and sectarian: Yoga, Sannyāsa, Vaiṣṇava, Śaiva, Śākta)
date_estimate: c. 800 BCE - c. 1500 CE (principal Upaniṣads ancient; many minor ones medieval)
summary: A large corpus of Upaniṣads, corresponding to the extended Muktikā-style canon (the 108 Upaniṣads of the Muktikopaniṣad list, here numbering 112 in this collection). Beyond the principal Upaniṣads it includes many later, often sectarian texts grouped as Sāmānya (Vedānta), Yoga, Sannyāsa, Vaiṣṇava, Śaiva, and Śākta Upaniṣads, dealing with meditation, renunciation, mantra, and devotion.
history: Whereas the principal Upaniṣads are late-Vedic, the many minor (gauṇa) Upaniṣads were composed over a long period, some as late as the medieval/early-modern era, and were gathered into canonical lists such as the 108 of the Muktikā Upaniṣad. They document the broadening of 'Upaniṣad' into a genre adopted by yogic and sectarian movements.

title: Chāndogya-Upaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional (Vedic seers)
date_estimate: c. 8th-6th c. BCE (one of the oldest Upaniṣads)
summary: One of the oldest and largest of the principal Upaniṣads, embedded in the Chāndogya-Brāhmaṇa of the Sāmaveda. In eight chapters it teaches through dialogue and parable the nature of Brahman and ātman, the significance of the syllable Oṃ (udgītha), meditation, and the unity of the self with ultimate reality. It contains the celebrated instruction of Uddālaka Āruṇi to his son Śvetaketu culminating in the mahāvākya 'tat tvam asi' ('that thou art').
history: A foundational source for Vedānta, heavily cited in the Brahmasūtra and commented upon by Śaṅkara and later teachers. Its dialogues (e.g. Satyakāma Jābāla, the doctrine of the five fires and the two paths) are among the most influential passages in Indian philosophy.

title: Chāndogya-Upaniṣad (Śaṅkarabhāṣya)
author: Śaṅkara (Ādi Śaṅkarācārya)
date_estimate: Śaṅkara's commentary c. early 8th c. CE; the root Upaniṣad c. 8th-6th c. BCE
summary: Śaṅkara's commentary on the Chāndogya-Upaniṣad, expounding the text passage by passage from the standpoint of non-dualist (Advaita) Vedānta. It interprets the Upaniṣad's teachings on Oṃ, the meditations, and especially the great identity statement 'tat tvam asi' as affirming the absolute non-difference of ātman and Brahman.
history: One of the major Upaniṣad-bhāṣyas attributed to Śaṅkara, it is a central document of classical Advaita exegesis and shaped the orthodox interpretation of the Chāndogya for the later tradition.

title: Kaṭha-Upaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional (Kaṭha school)
date_estimate: c. 5th-3rd c. BCE (one of the later principal Upaniṣads)
summary: A principal Upaniṣad of the Kaṭha school of the Black Yajurveda, framed as the dialogue between the boy Naciketas and Yama, the god of death. Through three boons Yama teaches Naciketas about fire-sacrifice, the immortal self (ātman), the contrast between the pleasant (preyas) and the good (śreyas), and the path to liberation, including the famous chariot allegory of the self, body, intellect, and senses.
history: Among the most poetically admired Upaniṣads, frequently commented upon (including by Śaṅkara) and widely translated. Its apparent acquaintance with ideas resembling Buddhist thought has been used to argue for a relatively late date among the principal Upaniṣads.

title: Āpastamba-Gṛhyasūtra
author: attributed to Āpastamba
tradition: Hindu (Vedic; Taittirīya school of the Black Yajurveda)
date_estimate: c. mid-1st millennium BCE (the Āpastamba corpus dated c. 600-300 BCE)
summary: The domestic-ritual manual (gṛhyasūtra) of the Āpastamba school of the Taittirīya (Black Yajurveda) tradition, forming part of the larger Āpastamba Kalpasūtra. In terse sūtra style it prescribes the household rites and rites of passage (saṃskāras): conception and childhood rites, upanayana (initiation), Vedic study, marriage, the daily and seasonal domestic offerings, and funerary observances.
history: It is closely related to the Hiraṇyakeśi-Gṛhyasūtra and gives only the ritual procedure, the accompanying mantras being collected separately in a Mantrapāṭha. The Āpastamba corpus (Śrauta-, Gṛhya-, and Dharmasūtra) is among the most important sources for the ritual life of South Indian Taittirīya brahmins.

title: Gobhila-Gṛhyasūtra
author: attributed to Gobhila
date_estimate: later Vedic / sūtra period, mid-to-late 1st millennium BCE
summary: The principal domestic-ritual manual (gṛhyasūtra) attached to the Sāmaveda, attributed to the sage Gobhila. It sets out the household rites and life-cycle saṃskāras—conception, birth, naming, tonsure, upanayana, marriage, śrāddha (ancestral offerings), and the regular domestic fire offerings—for followers of the Sāmaveda.
history: Classed among the Kalpasūtras, it is a key witness to Sāmavedic domestic practice and is often studied together with the Gṛhyasaṃgrahapariśiṣṭa and the Khādira-Gṛhyasūtra (a related, condensed text). It was translated by Hermann Oldenberg in the Sacred Books of the East.

title: Śāṅkhāyana-Gṛhyasūtra
author: attributed to Śāṅkhāyana (Kauṣītaki tradition)
date_estimate: later Vedic / sūtra period, mid-to-late 1st millennium BCE
summary: The domestic-ritual manual (gṛhyasūtra) of the Śāṅkhāyana (Kauṣītaki) school of the Ṛgveda, prescribing the household rites and rites of passage—initiation, Vedic study, marriage, daily and seasonal domestic offerings, and ancestral rites—for Ṛgvedins of this branch.
history: It is very closely related to the Kauṣītaki-Gṛhyasūtra, with the first four chapters showing extensive verbal agreement, and stands beside the Āśvalāyana-Gṛhyasūtra as one of the two Ṛgvedic domestic manuals. It belongs to the Kalpasūtra literature of the Kauṣītaki/Śāṅkhāyana lineage attached to the Ṛgveda.
